title: Bridge Essay: Impaired motor-to-sensory transformation mediates auditory hallucinations.
concept: Sense of agency — voluntary action, free will as constructed experience, intentional binding.

body.text: This bridge paper connects the esoteric/philosophical concept of **Nested Agency** with mechanistic science from PubMed/MEDLINE.
**Bridge rationale:** Nested agency — voluntary action, free will as constructed experience, intentional binding.
**Phase context:** Sense of agency — voluntary action, free will as constructed experience, intentional binding.

**Impaired motor-to-sensory transformation mediates auditory hallucinations.** — PLoS Biol (2024). DOI: 10.1371/journal.pbio.3002836. PMID: 39361912. Distinguishing reality from hallucinations requires efficient monitoring of agency. It has been hypothesized that a copy of motor signals, termed efference copy (EC) or corollary discharge (CD), suppresses sensory responses to yield a sense of agency; impairment of the inhibitory function leads to hallucinations. However, how can the sole absence of inhibition yield positive symptoms of hallucinations? We hypothesize that selective impairments in functionally distinct signals of CD and EC during
